_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.era_label: Founding of the People's Republic Era
station.tagline: She arrives without ever seeming to move — and somehow she's already in the room.

museum_objects.title: First Series Renminbi Note (1948)
museum_objects.description: The very first renminbi banknote, issued in Shijiazhuang, Hebei, with calligraphy by Dong Biwu.
museum_objects.significance: Mei arrived quietly in a provincial city, not a capital — and by the time anyone noticed, she was already the currency of the world's most populous nation.
museum_objects.plain_language: The very first version of China's currency, printed in 1948, a year before the People's Republic of China was even declared.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: RMB Steel Printing Plates (Complete Set)
museum_objects.description: The only complete set of original renminbi steel printing plates known to exist in the world.
museum_objects.significance: Every renminbi bill that has ever entered circulation traces back to plates like these — the literal fingerprint behind Mei's entire physical existence.
museum_objects.plain_language: The only complete set of the original metal plates used to print China's very first currency — nothing else like it exists.

museum_objects.title: 'Famous Running Horse' Rare Note
museum_objects.description: An extremely rare denomination of the first series renminbi, prized by collectors and auctioned by Spink.
museum_objects.significance: Most of these notes were destroyed or lost — the few that survive are treated almost like relics, evidence of how quickly and completely Mei's early history vanished.
museum_objects.plain_language: An extremely rare first-edition Chinese banknote, one of the most valuable Chinese currency collectibles in the world.

museum_objects.title: Shanghai, 1949 (Cartier-Bresson Photograph)
museum_objects.description: An iconic photograph by Henri Cartier-Bresson capturing the chaos and transition in Shanghai during the Communist takeover.
museum_objects.significance: This image is the human backdrop to Mei's rise — an entire society reorganizing itself in real time, right as her currency was becoming the only one that mattered.
museum_objects.plain_language: A famous photograph of Shanghai in 1949 showing the chaotic transition as China became a Communist state.

museum_objects.title: IMF SDR Basket Inclusion Announcement
museum_objects.description: The IMF's official announcement adding the Chinese renminbi to its Special Drawing Rights basket of reserve currencies.
museum_objects.significance: Nearly seventy years after arriving quietly in a provincial city, Mei was finally recognized by the world's most exclusive currency club — and she barely made a sound doing it.
museum_objects.plain_language: The official announcement from the world's top financial organization confirming China's currency had officially joined the world's most elite group of reserve currencies.

world_map.map_description: Mei was born quietly in Shijiazhuang in 1948, a year before the People's Republic was formally declared, and grew into one of the world's most closely watched reserve currencies.

nodes.label: Shijiazhuang, Hebei
nodes.description: Where the People's Bank of China was founded and the first renminbi notes were issued in December 1948.

nodes.label: Beijing (Tiananmen)
nodes.description: Site of Mao Zedong's proclamation of the People's Republic of China on October 1, 1949, formalizing the state Mei would represent.

nodes.label: Yan'an
nodes.description: The Communist Party's wartime revolutionary base, where much of the ideology behind the new currency system was developed.

nodes.label: Shanghai (Bank Museum)
nodes.description: China's financial capital, home to a museum preserving the unique original RMB printing plates.

nodes.label: Washington, D.C. (IMF)
nodes.description: Where the renminbi was formally recognized as a global reserve currency in 2016.

historical_map_source.title: Wikipedia — First Series of the Renminbi
